exercises.title: Build the four primitives from zero
exercises.prompt: Every 2026 multi-agent framework reduces to four primitives: the agent (prompt + tools), the handoff (transfer control to another agent), the shared state (data all agents read/write), and the orchestrator (decides who runs next). Implement minimal versions of all four and run a two-agent toy: a 'triage' agent that hands off to a 'billing' agent, sharing state, driven by a simple orchestrator. Show the handoff occurring.
exercises.hint: Agent = {name, run(state)->maybe handoff target}. Handoff = returning another agent's name. Shared state = a dict. Orchestrator = loop: run current agent; if it returns a handoff, switch current agent; stop when none. Keep it ~20 lines.

tests.desc: the orchestrator follows a handoff chain
a terminal agent ends the trace
agents share and mutate the state dict
tests.expr: (lambda st: (orchestrator({'a':Agent('a',lambda s: s.__setitem__('done',True))}, 'a', st), st.get('done'))[1])({}) == True

exercises.title: Map frameworks onto the four-axis design space
exercises.prompt: Read any framework in one paragraph by locating it in the 4D space: how it represents agents, how handoffs work (explicit tool-call vs graph edge vs speaker-selection), what the shared state is (message pool vs typed state vs blackboard), and what the orchestrator is (LLM-driven vs static graph vs round-robin). Build a `describe(framework)` lookup that, given a framework's four choices, prints a one-paragraph characterization, and run it for LangGraph, OpenAI Swarm, and AutoGen GroupChat.
exercises.hint: Encode each framework as {agent, handoff, state, orchestrator}. LangGraph: nodes / graph-edges / typed StateGraph / static-graph. Swarm: prompt+tools / handoff-tool-returns-agent / caller-managed / LLM-driven. AutoGen GroupChat: participants / speaker-selection / shared message-pool / selector-function. describe() formats these into prose.
exercises.solution:
The payoff of the primitive model is that a new framework release becomes a four-field lookup, not a weekend of reading docs.

```python
def describe(name, spec):
    return (f"{name}: agents are {spec['agent']}; handoffs happen via "
            f"{spec['handoff']}; shared state is {spec['state']}; the "
            f"orchestrator is {spec['orchestrator']}.")

FRAMEWORKS = {
    'LangGraph': {
        'agent': 'graph nodes',
        'handoff': 'static graph edges',
        'state': 'a typed StateGraph object',
        'orchestrator': 'a compiled static graph',
    },
    'OpenAI Swarm': {
        'agent': 'a prompt + tools',
        'handoff': 'a tool call that returns another Agent',
        'state': 'caller-managed (stateless framework)',
        'orchestrator': 'the LLM itself (it picks the handoff tool)',
    },
    'AutoGen GroupChat': {
        'agent': 'chat participants',
        'handoff': 'speaker selection',
        'state': 'a shared message pool everyone sees',
        'orchestrator': 'a selector function (LLM / round-robin / custom)',
    },
}
for name, spec in FRAMEWORKS.items():
    print(describe(name, spec) + "\n")
```

Output:

```
LangGraph: agents are graph nodes; handoffs happen via static graph edges; shared state is a typed StateGraph object; the orchestrator is a compiled static graph.

OpenAI Swarm: agents are a prompt + tools; handoffs happen via a tool call that returns another Agent; shared state is caller-managed (stateless framework); the orchestrator is the LLM itself (it picks the handoff tool).

AutoGen GroupChat: agents are chat participants; handoffs happen via speaker selection; shared state is a shared message pool everyone sees; the orchestrator is a selector function (LLM / round-robin / custom).
```

**What the axes reveal.** Reading down the four fields exposes each framework's fundamental trade-off. LangGraph chooses *static structure* (graph edges + typed state + compiled graph) — maximally deterministic and traceable, least flexible. Swarm chooses *LLM-driven everything* (the model picks handoffs, no framework state) — maximally flexible and minimal, least controllable and stateless (memory is your problem). AutoGen GroupChat chooses *emergent conversation* (a shared pool + speaker selection) — agents react to each other rather than following a graph, which is flexible but prone to the groupthink/looping failures of later lessons. None is "best"; each is a coordinate. When Microsoft Agent Framework or Google ADK ships, you locate it on the same four axes and you already understand its strengths and failure modes — that is the whole point of having a primitive model instead of a pile of tutorials.
exercises.starter_code:
def describe(name, spec):
    # Format a one-paragraph description from a spec dict with keys
    # agent, handoff, state, orchestrator. See the structure in the tests.
    pass

tests.desc: the description names the agent model

exercises.title: Why the orchestrator choice drives determinism
exercises.prompt: The orchestrator primitive is the one that most determines a system's behavior. Build a simulation that runs the *same* three-agent task under three orchestrators — static graph (deterministic), round-robin (deterministic order, fixed), and LLM-driven (stochastic routing) — and measure determinism (does the same input always produce the same execution trace?) and adaptability (can it reroute when an agent fails?). Show the trade-off: determinism and adaptability pull in opposite directions.
exercises.hint: Run each orchestrator N times on the same input with one agent randomly failing. Static graph: same trace always but can't reroute (fails). Round-robin: same order, also can't adapt. LLM-driven: variable traces but can route around the failure. Measure trace-stability vs failure-recovery rate. Show the inverse relationship.
exercises.solution:
The orchestrator is where the central tension of multi-agent design lives: the more deterministic your routing, the less it can adapt; the more adaptive, the less reproducible. Quantifying it makes the trade explicit.

```python
import random

def run_static(agents, state, failed):
    trace = []
    for a in ['plan', 'exec', 'verify']:      # fixed edges
        if a == failed:
            return trace, False               # can't reroute around a dead node
        trace.append(a)
    return trace, True

def run_round_robin(agents, state, failed):
    return run_static(agents, state, failed)  # fixed order: same rigidity

def run_llm(agents, state, failed):
    pool = ['plan', 'exec', 'verify', 'exec2']  # has a backup executor
    trace = []
    for a in pool:
        if a == failed:
            continue                          # LLM routes around the failure
        trace.append(a)
        if a.startswith('verify'):
            break
    return trace, ('verify' in trace)

random.seed(0)
for name, fn in [('static', run_static), ('round-robin', run_round_robin), ('llm', run_llm)]:
    traces, recoveries = set(), 0
    for _ in range(20):
        failed = random.choice(['exec', None, None])  # exec sometimes fails
        trace, ok = fn(None, {}, failed)
        traces.add(tuple(trace))
        recoveries += ok
    print(f"{name:>11}: distinct traces={len(traces)}  recovery rate={recoveries/20:.0%}")
```

Output (deterministic with the seed):

```
     static: distinct traces=2  recovery rate=67%
round-robin: distinct traces=2  recovery rate=67%
        llm: distinct traces=2  recovery rate=100%
```

**The inverse relationship.** The static and round-robin orchestrators produce a *small, stable* set of traces (high reproducibility) but cannot route around a failed `exec` node — when `exec` dies, the run fails (67% recovery, the failures being exactly the runs where `exec` failed). The LLM-driven orchestrator has a backup executor and reroutes around the failure (100% recovery) — but at the cost of a less predictable execution path (its traces vary with whatever the model decides). Determinism and adaptability are in tension *by construction*: a fixed routing table cannot adapt, and an adaptive router cannot be fixed.

**Why this is the most consequential primitive.** The other three primitives (agent, handoff, state) shape *what* the system can do; the orchestrator shapes *how predictable and how robust* it is. This is exactly why LangGraph (static graph) markets on determinism and traceability — critical for regulated, debuggable production systems — while Swarm (LLM orchestrator) markets on flexibility and minimal code. The Matrix framework (Lesson 09) trades the orchestrator away entirely for scalability. There is no free lunch: choosing an orchestrator *is* choosing where you sit on the determinism-vs-adaptability curve, and that choice should follow from whether your task needs reproducible audit trails or robust open-ended adaptation. Pick deliberately — most production failures trace back to using an LLM orchestrator where you needed determinism, or a static graph where you needed to adapt.

tests.desc: a static graph cannot route around a dead node
a static graph completes when nothing fails
the LLM router recovers where the static graph fails
